---
title: "Native Time and Clocks"
subtitle: "Keep feature timing lossless until a downstream model chooses a grid."
sidebar: concepts
---

## What it is

A temporal coordinate is meaningful only together with its clock and support.
`ClockRef` names the clock, `ClockMap` relates two clocks, `SupportSpec` says
whether a row is a point or interval, and `TemporalContext` holds a consistent
graph of clock mappings.

`FeatureBundle` groups different native grids without forcing them into one
table. Its `temporal_payload()` method is the handoff boundary for downstream
modeling.

## Invariants

- Every temporal object declares a clock through `timebase.reference`.
- A clock map means `target = scale * source + offset_s`.
- Contradictory paths through a temporal context are rejected.
- Clock conversion preserves values and row count.
- Window and interval support survives conversion and storage.

## What it refuses

- Implicit cross-clock alignment.
- Silent resampling to the fastest feature grid.
- Wide joins across clocks without an explicit target and policy.
- Treating TR or HRF settings as properties of a native feature.

## What it doesn't do

The clock layer does not convolve an HRF, interpolate onto TRs, or build a
design matrix. Those choices belong to downstream analysis libraries.

## See also

[Native temporal contract](../spec/native-temporal-contract.qmd) · [Keep heterogeneous features on native grids](../cookbook/native-grid-handoff.qmd) · [Feature objects](feature-objects.qmd)
